coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2 adhered on TiO2 nanoparticles: Impressively boosted photocatalytic efficiency for removal of pollutants under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Shafafi, Sara
Habibi-Yangjeh, Aziz
Feizpoor, Solmaz
Ghosh, Srabanti
Maiyalagan, Thandavarayan
coredata.dc:description: To expand efficient photocatalysts for water purification, carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2 were decorated on TiO2 nanoparticles via a facile method to construct high-efficiency TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalysts. The photoelectrochemical, textural, optical, structural, and morphological characteristics of the nanocomposites were studied via various techniques. The photocatalytic experiments confirmed that the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst with 20 wt% of Bi4O5Br2 has the greatest ability in elimination of MO, fuchsine, and RhB, which is almost 24.3, 30.7, and 31.5-folds premier than the bare TiO2 and 2.9, 1.8, and 2.4-folds as much as the TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) nanocomposite, respectively. This boosted photoability in the ternary photocatalyst was ascribed to the synergistic interactions between TiO2, Cdots, and Bi4O5Br2, more effective charge transportation, and enhancing the absorption of visible light. The trapping experiments revealed that the important species in the degradation process are h+ and •O2 −. Besides, the fabricated ternary photocatalyst showed acceptable stability. This study supplies new idea to the construction of visible-light-induced photocatalysts based on TiO2 for efficient degradation of water contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2
Visible-light-induced photocatalyst
Heterojunction photocatalyst
Water purification

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for the as-fabricated photocatalysts.
(a) TEM and (b) HRTEM images related to the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) powder.
XPS spectra of the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample: (a) survey scan and high resolution scans for (b) Ti, (c) Bi, (d) O, (e) Br, and (f) C.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) optical absorbances for the photocatalysts.
(a) RhB degradation on the fabricated samples. Absorption spectra for RhB over the (b) TiO2, (c) TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL), and (d) TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) samples.
(a) The degradation rate constants and (b) The BET surface area plots for the photocatalysts.
(a) Photocurrent responses, (b) EIS Nyquist plots, and (c) PL emission spectra for the as-fabricated photocatalysts.
The removal kinetics of RhB on the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample in the presence of various trapping agents.
Mott–Schottky curves for (a) TiO2 and (b) Bi4O5Br2 samples. (c) Plot of (ahv)2 versus hv for TiO2, and (d) Plot of (ahv)1/2 versus hv for Bi4O5Br2.
Mechanism of photocatalytic reaction on the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst.
Cycling elimination tests of RhB over the Bi4O5Br2 and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) photocatalysts.
The degradation rate constants of MO and fuchsine on the fabricated photocatalysts.
Schematic plot for synthesis of TiO₂/Cdots/Bi₄O₅Br₂ nanocomposites.
Textural properties of the TiO2, TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL), and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) photocatalysts.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2 adhered on TiO2 nanoparticles: Impressively boosted photocatalytic efficiency for removal of pollutants under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic and Inorganic Chemistry, Universidad De Alcala, Madrid, Spain

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, SRM Research Institute, SRM University, Kattankulathur 603203, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Faculty of Science, University of Mohaghegh Ardabili, P.O. Box 179, Ardabil, Iran.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To expand efficient photocatalysts for water purification, carbon dots and Bi4O5Br2 were decorated on TiO2 nanoparticles via a facile method to construct high-efficiency TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalysts. The photoelectrochemical, textural, optical, structural, and morphological characteristics of the nanocomposites were studied via various techniques. The photocatalytic experiments confirmed that the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst with 20 wt% of Bi4O5Br2 has the greatest ability in elimination of MO, fuchsine, and RhB, which is almost 24.3, 30.7, and 31.5-folds premier than the bare TiO2 and 2.9, 1.8, and 2.4-folds as much as the TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) nanocomposite, respectively. This boosted photoability in the ternary photocatalyst was ascribed to the synergistic interactions between TiO2, Cdots, and Bi4O5Br2, more effective charge transportation, and enhancing the absorption of visible light. The trapping experiments revealed that the important species in the degradation process are h+ and •O2 −. Besides, the fabricated ternary photocatalyst showed acceptable stability. This study supplies new idea to the construction of visible-light-induced photocatalysts based on TiO2 for efficient degradation of water contaminants.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is a worthy and fundamental natural source for the existence of life on earth. With the increase of industrialization and mankind population, a vast content of contaminants such as pharmaceutical compounds, organic dyes, and heavy metal ions are introduced into the groundwater. Organic dyes, as a significant part of water pollutions, are threatening the environment and human health [1–5]. Therefore, choosing suitable approach to remove these contaminants from the surface waters is a very critical issue. Semiconductor-assisted photocatalysis is realized to be the most favorable approach in the field of contaminant removal, comparing with traditional purification procedures [6–13]. Among various semiconductors, TiO2 is the most usable semiconductor in photocatalytic processes, owing to its wonderful advantages such as high stability, nontoxicity, and abundance. Despite its advantages, practical utilization of TiO2 has limited by its fast recombination of the photogenerated charge carriers and its wide band gap [14–19]. Therefore, in order to overcome these drawbacks, various strategies have been employed and many efforts have been dedicated to improve the photocatalytic performance of TiO2 [20–25].

Recently, carbon dots (Cdots) have been remarkably considered as an active component for photocatalysis, because of their optoelectronic and green features. These carbon-containing materials can broaden the light usage range with the up-conversion feature. They can absorb lights with large wavelengths and emit small wavelengths [26–30]. Due to this property, Cdots can be a useful candidate for boosting the photocatalytic capability of TiO2. Bi-based photocatalysts such as Bi2MoO6, BiVO4, Bi2S3, Bi2WO6, BiOBr, and BiOI are good candidates for photocatalysis applications due to their suitable band gap and considerable visible-light absorption [31–39]. Recently, Bi-rich bismuth oxybromides like Bi5O7Br, Bi12O17Br2, Bi3O4Br, Bi24O31Br10, and Bi4O5Br2 have aroused great interest in the field of photocatalytic processes, because of superior harvesting of visible light and low recombination of the charges [40–44]. Bi4O5Br2 is a favorable bismuth oxybromide photocatalyst due to its visible-light-responsive ability, unique layered structure, and appropriate band gap [45–47]. Hence, this semiconductor could contribute in the improvement of visible-light absorption of TiO2 after heterojunction formation. Although various attempts have been made to synthesis TiO2-based photocatalysts, preparation of TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalysts and investigation of the photocatalytic performance have not been reported.

Herein, we successfully prepared novel TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 samples for photodegradation of organic dyes. The electrochemical, textural, morphological, chemical, purity, and optical properties of the resultant photocatalysts were checked by EIS, PL, photocurrent density, BET, TEM, HRTEM, XPS, XRD, and UV–vis DRS techniques. The photocatalytic abilities were evaluated by degradations of three organic pollutants upon visible light. The possible mechanism for boosted photocatalytic ability of the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 nanocomposites was investigated. Lastly, the stability of the optimum nanocomposite was investigated via successive recycling tests.

The morphological features were checked via a TOPCON (EM-002B) microscope. The XPS data were collected by a JEOL (JPS-9010MC) photoelectron spectrometer. By using a diffractometer (Philips Xpert) with Cu Kα radiation, the XRD patterns of the samples was presented. FT-IR spectra were achieved by a Perkin Elmer SpectrumRX I spectrophotometer. A Belsorp Mini II instrument was utilized for the investigation of the porosity of the samples. The PL data were collected via Perkin Elmer LS 55. The optical properties of the samples were studied by a Scinco 4100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The ultrasonic treatment of the materials was performed by a Bandelin ultrasound generator of HD 3100. A µAutolabIII Potentiostat/Galvanostat was used to investigate the photoelectrochemical features. More information has been offered in our recent work [48].

The TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) powder was fabricated according to our previous report [49]. The TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalysts with several weight percentage of Bi4O5Br2 were obtained using the following method. In the synthesis of TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) photocatalyst, 0.4 g TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) photocatalyst was kept in ultrasonic treatment for 10 min to completely disperse the powder into 100 mL H2O. Then, the solution containing 0.18 g bismuth nitrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) and water (20 mL) were added into the above suspension. After 1 h stirring, 20 mL aqueous solution including 0.07 g cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was gradually poured into the above suspension. Then, the pH value adjusted to 8 with adding NaOH. After 30 min stirring, the solution was autoclaved at 180 °C for 12 h. After hydrothermal treatment, the synthesized sample was centrifuged, washed and dried (scheme 1 ).

The photocatalysis experiments were studied by elimination of three organic pollutants (fuchsine, RhB, and MO) under a visible-light source (50 W LED lamp) at 25 °C. The primary concentration of 1 × 10−5 mol/L was selected for MO and RhB, while 0.78 × 10−5 mol/L was used for fuchsine. In each test, 100 mg of the photocatalyst append to the pollutant solution and put in the dark to attain balance, and then the light switched on. After light exposure, the photocatalysts were collected from the glass reactor at specified times. The extent of the residue pollutant was supervised through a UV–vis spectrophotometer at the defined wavelengths.

The TEM and HRTEM analyses were carried out to study the morphology of the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) nanocomposite. From the TEM image, it can be observed that the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) photocatalyst possesses irregular sphere-like morphology. Fig. 2 b presents HRTEM image of the ternary sample, demonstrating that the Bi4O5Br2 and Cdots nanoparticles successfully dispersed on the surface of the TiO2 nanoparticles. The fringe spacing of 0.260 and 0.320 nm can be related to the (0 2 2) crystal plane of Bi4O5Br2 and (0 0 2) crystal plane of Cdots, respectively [47,48]. In addition, the fringe spacing of 0.352 nm is assigned to the (1 0 1) crystal plane of TiO2 [49]. These observations matched well with the XRD data, confirming synthesis of the ternary photocatalyst.

The XPS analysis was implemented to study the elemental composition and chemical environment of the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) photocatalyst. Based on the survey scanning spectrum (Fig. 3 a), the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) nanocomposite contained Ti, Bi, C, O, and Br elements. In Fig. 3b, the Ti 2p spectrum displays two peaks at 458.4 and 464.1 eV from Ti 2p3/2 and Ti 2p1/2, respectively, affirming that Ti is in the form of Ti4+ [53]. From the spectrum of Bi 4f (Fig. 3c), the peaks at 164.5 and 159.2 eV belonged to the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 of Bi3+ species [45]. The XPS spectrum of O 1s at 531.60 and 530.15 eV (Fig. 3d) are assigned to the hydroxyl group and lattice oxygen, respectively [54]. Br 3d in Fig. 3e indicates binding energies of 69.4 eV (Br 3d3/2) and 68.5 eV (Br 3d5/2), which can be attributed to Br− [50]. The peaks at 288.8 and 285.01 eV in C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3f) are related to the CO and CC groups from Cdots [48].

The functional groups and chemical structures of the synthesized samples were ascertained by FT-IR analysis, and the related spectra are illustrated in Fig. 4 a. The absorption bands at 3400–3800 and 500–700 cm−1 are assigned to the OH and TiOTi stretching vibrations [21]. The absorptions at about 1610 and 1415 cm−1 are attributed to the CO and CC bonds in the spectra of TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) nanocomposites [54]. For the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample, the band observed at 550 cm−1 could be ascribed to Bi-O stretching vibration [47]. The optical response of the as-obtained samples were investigated by UV–vis DRS measurements and the spectra are shown in Fig. 4b. The visible-light response of TiO2 was restricted with a given absorption edge at about 398 nm. It is obvious that the binary TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) powder could be excited by visible light, and more photoinduced electrons and holes could be formed in this photocatalyst. Notably, due to the coupling of TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) with Bi4O5Br2 semiconductor, the ternary TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 samples showed broader light absorption in almost the whole visible-light spectrum, representing their potential for impressive photocatalytic performance.

The degradation of RhB under visible light was conducted to study the photocatalytic capability of the obtained photocatalysts. It can be observed from Fig. 5 a that RhB is a very stable dye, because its self-photodegradation is very poor under the light illumination. Besides, the pristine TiO2 didn’t show observable changes in the photodegradation of RhB. In contrast, Bi4O5Br2 shows good activity, which degrades about 55.8% of RhB after the light irradiation for 90 min. The photodegradation value of RhB attained 62.1% by using the TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) sample after 90 min irradiation, which was greater than the TiO2 sample. After introducing Bi4O5Br2 nanoparticles to fabricate ternary TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 samples, the photocatalytic performance was outstandingly improved. In addition, the weight percentage of Bi4O5Br2 affects the photocatalytic ability of ternary photocatalysts and with the increment of Bi4O5Br2 content, the degradation of RhB first boosts and then reduces and the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) nanocomposite shows the highest removal efficiency, compared with the other photocatalysts. The improved photocatalytic performance for ternary photocatalysts could be related to the forming of heterojunction between TiO2, Cdots, and Bi4O5Br2 nanoparticles and enhancing the absorption of visible light. The temporal variation of the UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB at 553 nm over the TiO2, TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL), and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) photocatalysts are presented in Fig. 5 (b-d). For all samples, with the increase of irradiation time, the intensity of absorption peaks was reduced. As seen, the absorption peaks of RhB diminished to about 21.2, 62.1, and 99.8% within 90 min over the TiO2, TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL), and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) samples, respectively.

The degradation kinetic of RhB on the samples originates from pseudo-first-order model. As seen in Fig. 6 a, the amounts of rate constants over the TiO2, Bi4O5Br2, TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL), and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) samples are 14.0 × 10−4, 173 × 10−4, 182 × 10−4, and 441 × 10−4 min−1, respectively. Consequently, the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) nanocomposite showed the superior photodegradation performance, which is almost 31.5 and 2.4-folds premier than the bare TiO2 and TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) photocatalysts, respectively. In this part, the porosity of the samples was examined by N2 sorption analysis, as depicted in Fig. 6b and Table 1 . The isotherms of the TiO2, TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL), and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) samples are consistent with the type-IV isotherms, which revealed the mesoporous structure. Based on data from Table 1, the surface area of TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) photocatalyst (53.4 m2g−1) is slightly larger than the pure and binary samples. The incorporation of Bi4O5Br2 and Cdots nanoparticles on the TiO2 surface can supply more active centers for the degradation reaction, which improve the photocatalytic performance.

Because of the direct effect of recombination/separation of charge carries on the efficiency of photocatalytic reactions, we used photocurrent density, EIS, and PL analyses to gain more insights about the photocatalytic reactions. Transient photocurrent measurements of the TiO2, TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL), and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) samples are depicted in Fig. 7 a. The order of photocurrent intensities were followed TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) > TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) > TiO2. The highest photocurrent intensity in the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample suggests the lowest recombination rate of photogenerated carrier and the highest photocatalytic efficiency. To further confirm the separation of electron/hole pairs, EIS analyses were conducted. As shown in Fig. 7b, the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample has the smallest arc in the EIS semi circles, manifesting the lowest charge migration resistance and premier photocatalytic ability. The charge transfer efficiency was investigated according to the emission intensity in the PL spectra. As seen in Fig. 7c, the weakest PL emission intensity for the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) nanocomposite implicates the efficient mobility and separation of the carrier charges. Thus, the PL, EIS, and photocurrent outcomes indicated that the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample possesses the lowest recombination for the photoinduced charge carriers, consistent with the outcomes of the photocatalytic experiments.

To specify the effect of reactive radical species on the photocatalytic activity, the trapping experiments were carried out in the presence of diverse trapping agents (Fig. 8 ). Ammonium oxalate (AO) as a quencher for h+, benzoquinone (BQ) as a quencher for •O2 −, and 2-propanol (2-PrOH) as a quencher for •OH were added into the photocatalytic reaction system. By evaluating the results, one can realize that the removal of RhB on the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) nanocomposite was restrained considerably upon the introduction of BQ and AO, while the removal rate of RhB was reduced slightly after introduction of 2-PrOH. It can be deduced that •O2 − and h+ play substantial roles in the photocatalytic reaction.

Mott-Schottky (M−S) curve is ordinarily used to study the type of semiconductors and the flat band position (Vfb). The positive slope of the straight lines suggests n-type semiconductor. As displayed in Fig. 9 (a, b), the flat band potentials of n-type TiO2 and n-type Bi4O5Br2 have been estimated to be −0.60 and −0.27 V vs. Ag/AgCl (-0.40 and −0.07 V vs. NHE) [55]. The difference between the conduction band potential of an n-type semiconductor with the flat band potentials is about 0.1 V [56]. Hence, the corresponding conduction band of Bi4O5Br2 and TiO2 are estimated to be −0.17 and −0.50 V. Based on the Tauc's plots, the energy gaps of Bi4O5Br2 and TiO2 are +2.62 and +3.20 eV, respectively. Besides, the valance band of Bi4O5Br2 and TiO2 are estimated to be +2.45 and +2.70 V using the empirical formula of EVB = ECB + Eg.

The feasible mechanism for RhB degradation on the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 nanocomposites is presented in Fig. 10 . As observed, an n-n heterojunction was constructed at the interface of TiO2 and Bi4O5Br2, semiconductors. The electrons are migrated from TiO2 to Bi4O5Br2, owing to its negative EF. To achieve equilibrium in the Fermi levels, the band position of TiO2 moves down to the positive potential and Bi4O5Br2 moves up to the negative potential. When the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 photocatalyst exposed to visible light, Bi4O5Br2 can excite to generate charges under visible-light illumination. Besides, Cdots with an up conversion feature can excite TiO2 to generate electrons and holes. Cdots convert longer wavelength lights to shorter wavelength lights for excitation of TiO2 [48]. Finally electrons on the CB of Bi4O5Br2 could inject to the CB of TiO2, simultaneously the holes can migrate from the VB of TiO2 to that of Bi4O5Br2. In this mechanism, the recombination of charge carriers impressively inhibited. Therefore, the separated charge carriers can participate in the oxidation of pollutants and water molecules, leading to promoted photocatalytic ability.

The reusability of photocatalysts is a momentous character for industrial applications. Therefore, the photocatalytic experiments of Bi4O5Br2 and TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) nanocomposite were conducted for four consecutive runs. As depicted in Fig. 11 , in spite of the Bi4O5Br2, the removal efficiency of RhB by the ternary nanocomposite has no significant diminish after four recycles. According to the results, the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample can be regarded as a stable photocatalyst in the environmental applications.

To demonstrate high capability of the as-obtained photocatalysts in the photocatalytic processes, the degradation of MO and fuchsine was also surveyed. According to Fig. 12 , the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample illustrated premier photocatalytic capacity in the elimination of these dyes. The photoactivity of the TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) sample is 2.9 and 1.8-folds faster than the TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) nanocomposite and 24.3 and 30.7-folds larger than the TiO2 powder towards MO and fuchsine degradations, respectively. Hence, the photocatalytic outcomes indicated that the fabricated photocatalyst possesses superior ability in degradations of various organic pollutants.

Briefly, novel TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 hybrids were constructed to degrade organic contaminants in the presence of visible light. The characterizations proved that Cdots and Bi4O5Br2 nanoparticles successfully decorated on the TiO2 nanoparticles. The ternary TiO2/Cdots/Bi4O5Br2 (20%) photocatalyst exhibited the superior photoability in the degradations of MO, fuchsine, and RhB, which is almost 24.3, 30.7, and 31.5-folds premier than the bare TiO2 and 2.9, 1.8, and 2.4-folds as high as the TiO2/Cdots (0.5 mL) nanocomposite, respectively. The scavenger experiments clearly confirmed that h+ and •O2 − species have significant effects in the degradation process. The outcomes of the recyclability tests also demonstrated that the obtained photocatalyst possessed great stability, which can be considered as beneficial photocatalyst to be utilized in the photocatalytic reactions.

Sara Shafafi: Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Resources, Writing - original draft. Aziz Habibi-Yangjeh: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Solmaz Feizpoor: Writing - original draft, Methodology. Srabanti Ghosh: Writing - review & editing, Formal analysis. Thandavarayan Maiyalagan: Formal analysis.
The financial support of present research by University of Mohaghegh Ardabili-Iran is acknowledged.
This research is compliance with Ethical Standards.
Authors declare that there is not any conflict of interest.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A critical review on textile wastewater treatments: Possible approaches
J. Environ. Manage.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Systematic review on applicability of magnetic iron oxides–integrated photocatalysts for degradation of organic pollutants in water
